How to get a pile of documents processed for machine learning


NICAR 2025
Time: Saturday, March 7: 10:15 a.m.-11:15 a.m.
Location: Room 205, Mac lab
ver. 3/4/2026.

Ever wanted to use software to analyze a stack of PDFs or Word documents but you don’t know how to start? This session is for you. Using R, I will walk through the process of processing dozens of PDF documents into machine-readable text. Setting this up correctly saves you time but expands the analytical opportunities down the road. We will then walk through a quick tour of common text analysis strategies. This session is for adventurous beginners and people who can open R, load data and not freak out.


Rob Wells, Ph.D.
Philip Merrill College of Journalism, University of Maryland.
robwells@umd.edu | LinkedIn.

Coach: Aidan Hughes
Data Journalist, Howard Center for Investigative Journalism.
Philip Merrill College of Journalism, University of Maryland.
athughes@umd.edu | LinkedIn.

Credits:

  • Sean Mussenden, interim director, Howard Center for Investigative Journalism.

Load software

#Install packages if you don't have them already
# install.packages("tidyverse")
# install.packages("tidytext")
# install.packages("quanteda")
# install.packages("readtext")
# install.packages("DT")
# install.packages("rio")
# install.packages("lubridate")
# install.packages("zoo")
#install.packages("pdftools")
library(tidyverse)
library(pdftools)
library(janitor)
library(striprtf)
library(tidytext) 
library(quanteda)
library(readtext)
library(DT)
library(lubridate)
library(rio)
library(zoo)

Data transformation #1: rich text files

We’re going to take a folder of text files and compile them into a single dataframe for analysis.

We are working with a folder full of news articles about a particular journalist. I downloaded them from NexisUni, a common news database widely used by colleges and newsrooms that are not on a starvation budget.

The search of “Raymond Moley” and atleast2(Moley) returned 87 rich text file (.rtf) documents. We downloaded the individual files and then separately downloaded a spreadsheet with the metadata of the files. We’ll attach the metadata to the downloaded files.

First step, we have to please the R gods by converting the .rtf files into .txt files.

Do not anger the R gods!

Steps:

  • Create a folder Text_Compiler

  • And inside that folder, create an input folder and an output folder

  • Copy your spreadsheet index and the folder of text files into Text_Compiler folder

  • Create an .Rproj file for Text_Compiler

The 87 news articles are in a folder called input.

Reformat .RTF files to raw text

This chunk reads each individual .rtf file, processes it in a loop and transforms it to a .txt raw text file

Next, we take all of those text files and jam them into a dataframe –> This is a very big deal <-

It allows you to perform advanced text analytics on the news stories

Raw text compiler

Check for duplicates

Zap duplicates

Article count drops from 87 to 68

Check for duplicate entries

final_data |> 
  count(title) |> 
  arrange(desc(n)) |> 
  filter(n > 1)
## # A tibble: 0 × 2
## # ℹ 2 variables: title <chr>, n <int>

Final index

We add the file path for our processed text to permit compiling

final_index <- final_data |> 
  inner_join(files, c("index")) |> 
#you need the actual hard-coded path on this line below to the text
  mutate(filepath = paste0("./output/", filename))

Text compiler

Now we process those articles into a single spreadsheet.

Quick view of any entry by row

wrapped_text <- strwrap(as.character(articles_final$full_text[2]), width = 60)
writeLines(wrapped_text, "temp_text.txt")
#file.edit("temp_text.txt")

Clean data

We saw a lot of metadata at the bottom of the article. We’ll strip it out so we have clean text for analysis

articles_df <- articles_final |>
  mutate(
    # 1. Strip everything BEFORE the word "Body"
    # This looks for "Body" (case insensitive) and removes everything preceding it
    clean_text = sub("(?i).*?Body\\s+", "", full_text),
    
    # 2. Strip everything AFTER the metadata markers
    # This looks for the first instance of ©, "Classification", or "Load-Date" 
    # and deletes that and everything following it.
    clean_text = sub("(?i)(\u00a9|Classification|Load-Date|End of Document).*", "", clean_text),
    
    # 3. Final polish: remove any leftover trailing whitespace
    clean_text = trimws(clean_text)
  ) |>
  # Optional: Remove the original messy full_text and keep the clean one
  select(filename, published_date, headline, clean_text, everything(), -full_text) |> 
  rename(full_text = clean_text)

Deal with dates

Quick view of any entry by row

wrapped_text <- strwrap(as.character(articles_final$full_text[6]), width = 60)
writeLines(wrapped_text, "temp_text.txt")
#file.edit("temp_text.txt")

Extracting text from PDFs

Convert PDF to text Using 10 articles downloaded from NexisUni for Kemi Busari’s research

#Using pdftools package. Good for basic PDF extraction
text <- pdf_text("./input/kemi.PDF")
#pdf_text reads the text from a PDF file.
writeLines(text, "./output/kemi_text.txt")
#writeLines writes this text to a text file

Split text to separate articles on common identifier

In this case, NexisUni makes life easy for us. At the end of each document, there are the words “End of Document”. Convenient! We search for “End of Document” and then instruct R to split the file and dump it into a standalone text file.

# Step 1: Read the entire text file into R
#You will need to alter this for your computer
#For Mac: In Finder, Cntl + click on the filename, NOW hold down Alt/Option, and an item to copy file path will appear as Copy "Filename" as Pathname 
#https://stackoverflow.com/questions/52695546/how-to-copy-path-of-a-file-in-mac-os


file_path <- "./output/kemi_text.txt"
text_data <- readLines(file_path)

# Step 2: Combine lines into one single string
text_combined <- paste(text_data, collapse = "\n")

# Step 3: Split the text by the "End of Document" phrase
documents <- strsplit(text_combined, "End of Document")[[1]]

# Step 4: Write each section to a new file
output_dir <- "./pdf_extracted_text/"
for (i in seq_along(documents)) {
  output_file <- file.path(output_dir, paste0("kemi_extracted_", i, ".txt"))
  writeLines(documents[[i]], output_file)
}

cat("Files created:", length(documents), "\n")
## Files created: 11

Create an index from the first extracted page

–We are just grabbing the index of the 10 listed items and creating a dataframe

kemi_index <- read_lines("./pdf_extracted_text/kemi_extracted_1.txt")
# Extract lines 16 to 58
extracted_lines <- kemi_index[16:58]


# Print the extracted lines to the console
cat(extracted_lines, sep = "\n")
##   1. After Dele Giwa, Dan Bello Takes the Stage -By Sale Rusulana Yanguruza
##   Aug 14, 2024      Opinion Nigeria          Opinion Nigeria
## 
## 
##   2. Court Orders FG To Investigate, Prosecute Killers Of Dele Giwa, Other Journalists
##   Feb 16, 2024      The Will (Nigeria)
## 
## 
##   3. UPDATED: Court orders FG to probe deaths of Dele Giwa, other journalists
##   Feb 16, 2024      The Punch
## 
## 
##   4. AFRICMIL invites young journalists to submit essays on late Dele Giwa
##   Sep 18, 2021      The Nation (Nigeria)
## 
## 
##   5. Dele Giwa: Not a vacuous judgment
##   Mar 07, 2024      The Nation (Nigeria)
## 
## 
##   6. Dele Giwa: 37 Years After, His Message Remains Valid
##   Oct 20, 2023      Osun Defender
## 
## 
##   7. Re: A Vacuous Judgment on Dele Giwa
##   Mar 06, 2024      Fresh Angle International Newspaper            Freshangle
## 
## 
## 
## 
##              | About LexisNexis | Privacy Policy | Terms & Conditions | Copyright © 2020 LexisNexis
## 
## 8. A Sound Judgment On Dele Giwa
## Mar 09, 2024      Daily Independent (Nigeria) IDOWU ADEWALE
## 
## 
## 9. Re: A Vacuous Judgment on Dele Giwa
## Mar 06, 2024      Fresh Angle International Newspaper            Freshangle
## 
## 
## 10. Court Orders AGF To Re-open Trial Of Dele Giwa's Killers
## Feb 16, 2024      Daily Independent (Nigeria) Paschal Njoku
extracted_lines <- extracted_lines |> 
  as.data.frame() 


extracted_lines <- extracted_lines |> 
  mutate(extracted_lines = str_remove(extracted_lines, "\\| About LexisNexis \\| Privacy Policy \\| Terms & Conditions \\| Copyright © 2020 LexisNexis"))

Build a final dataframe index

# Step 1: Trim spaces and detect rows with titles and dates
cleaned_data <- extracted_lines |>
  mutate(
    # Trim leading and trailing spaces before detection
    trimmed_line = str_trim(extracted_lines),  

    # Detect titles (start with a number and a period)
    is_title = str_detect(trimmed_line, "^\\d+\\. "),  

    # Detect dates (e.g., "Aug 14, 2024")
    is_date = str_detect(trimmed_line, "\\b\\w{3} \\d{1,2}, \\d{4}\\b")
  )

# Step 2: Shift dates to align with corresponding titles
aligned_data <- cleaned_data |>
  mutate(
    date = ifelse(lead(is_date, 1), lead(trimmed_line, 1), NA_character_)  # Shift date to title's row
  ) |>
  filter(is_title) |>
  select(trimmed_line, date)  # Keep only the relevant columns

# Step 3: Rename columns for clarity
final_data <- aligned_data |>
  rename(
    title = trimmed_line,
    date = date
  )

#Step 4: Date and Publication in separate columns, and formatted
final_data <- separate(data = final_data, col = date, into = c("date2", "publication"), sep = "  ", extra = "merge", fill = "right")


#Step 5: Format date, clean headline
final_data <- final_data |> 
  mutate(date = as.Date(date2,format = "%b %d, %Y")) |> 
  mutate(title =str_remove(title, "^\\d+\\. ")) |> 
  subset(select = -(date2)) |> 
  mutate(index = row_number()) |> 
  select(index, date, title, publication)

write_csv(final_data, "pdf_final_data.csv")

Repeat sequence above

Now you have a folder with raw text and a dataframe with metadata. You can now repeat the steps starting from text compiler and build a dataframe with clean text and perform basic computational text analysis

For more complicated PDFs, bring in the Big Guns

#Using the system() function to execute a command-line operation directly from within R.
# system("brew install tesseract")
# system("brew install xpdf")
# system("xcode-select --install")
# system("brew install libtiff")
# system("brew install ghostscript")
# system("brew install imagemagick")

Convert PDF to Text

#Executes pdftotext, a command-line tool used to convert PDF files to plain text.
# system("pdftotext kemi.PDF ./extracted_text/kemi_tesseract.txt")

Computational Text Analysis

Now that you have the material in raw text format and in a dataframe, let’s start digging in. Here’s a separate presentation I did Thursday that describes the process in some detail.

https://profrobwells.github.io/Guest_Lectures/NICAR_Text_Analysis_2026/NICAR_Text_analysis_2026.html

Bigrams

bigrams <- articles_df |> 
  select(full_text) |> 
  mutate(text = str_squish(full_text)) |> 
  mutate(text = tolower(text)) |>  
  mutate(text = str_replace_all(text, "- ", "")) |> 
  unnest_tokens(bigram, text, token="ngrams", n=2 ) |> 
  separate(bigram, c("word1", "word2"), sep = " ") |> 
  filter(!word1 %in% stop_words$word) |>
  filter(!word2 %in% stop_words$word) |> 
  count(word1, word2, sort = TRUE) |> 
  filter(!is.na(word1))

bigrams
## # A tibble: 12,323 × 3
##    word1     word2           n
##    <chr>     <chr>       <int>
##  1 raymond   moley          68
##  2 white     house          68
##  3 jonathan  alter          47
##  4 bob       schieffer      45
##  5 isabella  rossellini     42
##  6 peter     bogdanovich    39
##  7 social    security       33
##  8 hundred   days           26
##  9 inaugural address        25
## 10 supreme   court          25
## # ℹ 12,313 more rows

Top 20 bigrams

top_20_bigrams <- bigrams |> 
   top_n(20) |> 
  mutate(bigram = paste(word1, " ", word2)) |> 
  select(bigram, n)
## Selecting by n
top_20_bigrams
## # A tibble: 20 × 2
##    bigram                    n
##    <chr>                 <int>
##  1 raymond   moley          68
##  2 white   house            68
##  3 jonathan   alter         47
##  4 bob   schieffer          45
##  5 isabella   rossellini    42
##  6 peter   bogdanovich      39
##  7 social   security        33
##  8 hundred   days           26
##  9 inaugural   address      25
## 10 supreme   court          25
## 11 brain   trust            22
## 12 world   war              22
## 13 franklin   delano        21
## 14 adam   davidson          19
## 15 deposit   insurance      19
## 16 eric   rauchway          18
## 17 war   ii                 17
## 18 delano   roosevelt       16
## 19 federal   reserve        16
## 20 president   franklin     16

Create a chart

ggplot(top_20_bigrams, aes(x = reorder(bigram, n), y = n, fill=n)) +
  geom_bar(stat = "identity") +
  theme(legend.position = "none") +
  coord_flip() +  
  labs(title = "Top Two-Word phrases in Moley coverage",
       caption = "n=68 articles. Graphic by Rob Wells. 3-1-2026",
       x = "Phrase",
       y = "Count of terms")

Sentiment analysis

Basic

# load sentiment dictionary
afinn <- get_sentiments("afinn")

#tokenize the dataframe, grouping by article number
sentiment <- articles_df |> 
  select(full_text) |> 
  mutate(article_nmbr = row_number()) |> 
  mutate(text = str_squish(full_text)) |> 
  mutate(text = tolower(text)) |>  
  mutate(text = str_replace_all(text, "- ", "")) |> 
  group_by(article_nmbr) |> 
  unnest_tokens(tokens, text) |> 
  filter(!tokens %in% stop_words$word) 

# Sentiment analysis by joining the tokenized words with the AFINN lexicon
sentiment_analysis <- sentiment |>
  inner_join(afinn, by = c("tokens"="word")) |>
  group_by(article_nmbr) |>  
  summarize(sentiment = sum(value), .groups = "drop")

# aggregate at article level, total sentiment score (Positive or Negative)
sentiment_analysis <- sentiment_analysis |>
   group_by(article_nmbr) |> 
  mutate(sentiment_type = ifelse(sentiment >= 0, "Positive", "Negative"))

Examine Keywords, Phrases in Context


Process Text Corpus Object

# Create a unique document ID for each article
kwic_search <- articles_df |>
  mutate(doc_id = paste0("article_", row_number()))


my_corpus <- corpus(kwic_search, text_field = "full_text")

# Add metadata (optional but useful)
docvars(my_corpus)$date <- articles_df$date

Search for the term “media”

# Tokenize corpus
my_tokens <- tokens(my_corpus)

# KWIC search
kwic_media <- kwic(my_tokens, pattern = "media", valuetype = "fixed") |> 
  as.data.frame() |>
  mutate(doc_id = docname)

Search Journalism Terms

media_narratives <- kwic(my_tokens, 
                         pattern = c("media", "reporter", "newspaper", "journalist"), 
                         valuetype = "fixed") |> 
  as.data.frame() |>
  mutate(doc_id = docname)
media_narratives_table <- media_narratives |> 
  select(doc_id, pre, keyword, post)

datatable(media_narratives_table,
          caption = "Journalism Terms in Context",
          options = list(pageLength = 20))
